cards.text: ESSCOM marks 13 years protecting eastern Sabah. Zero kidnappings since 2020. But its forward base is 41% complete after a decade, and it still has no Act of Parliament.
cards.sub: On the anniversary of the 2013 Lahad Datu incursion — when approximately 200 armed Sulu militants invaded eastern Sabah, killing at least 68 people including 10 Malaysian security personnel — the Eastern Sabah Security Command (ESSCOM) still operates under executive instruments rather than a dedicated Act of Parliament. ESSCOM was formally established on 1 April 2013 following the 7 March Special Security Area declaration. The Auditor-General's 2025 report revealed the Pulau Mabul forward operating base — approved in 2016 — is only 41% complete, with costs rising from RM52.81 million to RM81.51 million after contractor failure and termination.
revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3): Changed '235 militants' to 'approximately 200' per SEARCCT primary source
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Changed '72 killed' to 'at least 68' — sources vary between 68-78; primary sources use different counts
CORRECTED (Stage 3): ESSCOM established 1 April 2013, not 7 March (which was the SSA declaration date)
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Original cost was RM52.81 million, not RM41 million per AG-derived reporting (Utusan Malaysia)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): Softened 'never codified into law' to 'operates under executive instruments rather than a dedicated Act' — MOHA's 2024 Executive Instruction No. 1 shows legal framework was updated
REMOVED (Stage 3): Dropped unverified claim about permanent bill being 'deferred through multiple parliamentary sessions' — no Hansard evidence found

cards.h: ESSCOM foiled 59 kidnapping attempts and achieved zero kidnap-for-ransom since January 2020 — but the decline also coincided with Philippine military operations against Abu Sayyaf and COVID-19 border closures.
cards.s: Over 12 years, ESSCOM detained 16,000+ undocumented migrants, expelled 500+ foreign fishing vessels, and eliminated kidnap-for-ransom incidents since January 2020. These figures come from ESSCOM anniversary reporting in April 2025, citing PPDJS adviser Mohd Zaki Harry Susanto. Australia lowered its eastern Sabah travel advisory from Level 3 to Level 2 in August 2025. The US rates Malaysia Level 1 overall, but eastern Sabah islands and maritime areas remain Level 2. However, the infrastructure underpinning operations is fragile: the Pulau Mabul forward operating base, approved in 2016, was only 41% complete when the Auditor-General reported in October 2025. The contractor withdrew in August 2023 and the contract was terminated. Costs rose from RM52.81 million to RM81.51 million. The Semporna maritime complex received a partial handover in November 2025, with bridge and jetty works due by mid-2026. The government is exploring a Mobile Offshore Base concept as an alternative to stalled static infrastructure.
cards.d: Sources: AG Report 2025 Series 3, PPDJS/ESSCOM (Apr 2025), BERNAMA (Mar 2025), CSIS AMTI (May 2025), KKR Malaysia (Nov 2025), Utusan Malaysia (Oct 2025).
revisions: ADDED (Stage 4): Philippine military operations against Abu Sayyaf and COVID-19 border closures as contributing factors to zero kidnappings — avoids overclaiming ESSCOM causation
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Attribution corrected — 16,000+ and 500+ figures from PPDJS April 2025, not Saifuddin March 2025
CORRECTED (Stage 3): US travel advisory corrected — Malaysia overall Level 1, but eastern Sabah islands/maritime remain Level 2 per Feb 2026 advisory
CORRECTED (Stage 3): FOB original cost corrected from RM41M to RM52.81M
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Semporna complex was partial handover, not completed — full works due mid-2026
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Softened MOB pivot — presented as government exploring rather than definitive shift

cards.h: Successive administrations — BN, then PH-led — have maintained ESSCOM under executive instruments for 13 years without passing a dedicated Act, while its legal framework was quietly updated in 2024.
cards.s: ESSCOM was established on 1 April 2013 by executive directive under BN's Najib administration. It has since operated under executive instructions and public-order regulations rather than a dedicated Act of Parliament. In February 2024, MOHA signed a new Executive Instruction No. 1, read together with the Public Order Preservation Regulations (Amendment) 2024, effective 1 March 2024 — showing the framework was updated but not formalised into legislation. The maritime curfew on ESSZone waters began on 16 July 2014 and was rebranded in 2023 as the ESS Zone MCO, now covering six districts with Tawau excluded. The curfew has been in effect for nearly 12 years — a measure that affects B40 fishermen who cannot operate after dark, and women who run small fish markets dependent on night catches. ESSCOM's 2024-2028 Strategic Plan requested RM42.93 million for 8 projects under the 13th Malaysia Plan, competing within the Home Ministry's RM21.2 billion 2026 allocation.
cards.d: Sources: MOHA official history, BERNAMA (curfew implementation Jul 2014), Borneo Post (Oct 2025), Astro Awani (strategic plan), The Star (Oct 2024).
revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3): Curfew started 16 July 2014, not 2013 — major factual correction
CORRECTED (Stage 3): ESSCOM established 1 April 2013 per MOHA
ADDED (Stage 3): MOHA's 2024 Executive Instruction No. 1 update — shows framework updated without legislation
ADDED (Stage 2/5): Named successive administrations (BN then PH-led) to avoid asymmetric blame on current government
ADDED (Stage 2/4): B40 fishermen and women's livelihood impact of curfew — addressing gender erasure and economic gaps
CORRECTED (Stage 5): Budget competition presented as analysis, not stated as fact

cards.h: Eastern Sabah's 1,400 km security zone operates alongside over 1 million non-citizens in the state — in a region where poverty runs at 19.5% versus the national 6.2%.
cards.s: ESSZone spans 10 districts from Kudat to Tawau. The Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM) estimates 1.04 million non-citizens in Sabah as of 2025 — a distinct figure from the higher advocacy estimates that combine undocumented migrants, stateless persons, and unregistered children of mixed heritage. While cross-border security risks from the Sulu archipelago persist, local communities of Sulu, Bajau, and Filipino origin face heightened scrutiny that affects livelihoods. Sabah's poverty rate of 19.5% in 2022 — more than triple the national 6.2% — means the economic roots of insecurity run deeper than any maritime patrol can address. Sabah leaders argue federal infrastructure and development commitments remain unfulfilled, while federal planners cite competing national priorities and the logistical challenges of remote infrastructure delivery where cost overruns are common. The 2013 incursion led Malaysia to stop paying the annual RM5,300 cession money to Sulu heirs — a payment maintained since 1963.
cards.d: Sources: DOSM (2025 non-citizen estimate), Borneo Post (Oct 2025), LSE Blog (Nov 2025), The Star (Dec 2025), Sabah poverty data from national statistics.
revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3): Changed '2 million undocumented' to DOSM's official 1.04 million non-citizens — distinguished from advocacy estimates
ADDED (Stage 2/5): Balanced Sabah-KL framing — included both Sabah leaders' argument and federal planners' perspective on competing priorities
ADDED (Stage 2): Reframed Sulu/Bajau/Filipino communities with agency — 'face heightened scrutiny' rather than passive 'securitised by it'
ADDED (Stage 4): Sabah poverty rate (19.5% vs 6.2%) as root economic context for insecurity
ADDED (Stage 5): Acknowledged cost overruns as partly structural (remote logistics) not purely governance failure

cards.h: The question is not whether ESSCOM works — it does. The question is why three successive administrations have kept a security command protecting 1,400 km of coastline on executive instruments rather than giving it the permanence its mission demands.
cards.text: Crisis-driven institutions that are never formalised tend to lose institutional memory and budget certainty. ESSCOM has not atrophied yet — its operational record is genuinely strong. But every year without a dedicated Act is another year where authority depends on executive renewal, budgets compete ad hoc, and oversight remains limited. The pattern holds across administrations: no government — BN, PN-aligned, or PH-led — has moved to legislate. That consistency suggests the obstacle is structural, not partisan.
revisions: STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Named the cross-administration pattern explicitly — BN, PN, PH all failed to legislate
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Removed specific claim about bill deferrals (unverified) — reframed as structural observation

cards.h: Think of it like a hospital built after an earthquake — saving lives for 13 years from a tent while the permanent building sits 41% complete
cards.text: ESSCOM was the emergency response. It worked. The patients survived. But 13 years later, the doctors are still operating out of the temporary field hospital because the permanent structure was never finished. If this were a security zone in the Klang Valley, the language would be 'national scandal.' In Sabah, it is framed as a 'regional grievance.' The tent holds — for now. But you don't build national security on a structure that was supposed to last 36 months.
revisions: ADDED (Stage 5): Incorporated shoe-test insight — if this were Klang Valley, language would be 'national scandal' not 'regional grievance'

cards.text: ESSCOM is a genuine security success operating on structurally fragile foundations. Zero kidnappings since 2020, 59 attempts foiled, 16,000+ undocumented migrants detained — real achievements by real personnel, though the decline in kidnappings also reflects Philippine military pressure on Abu Sayyaf and COVID-era border closures. The institution delivering these results has no dedicated Act of Parliament, its forward base is 41% complete after a decade at 54% over original cost, and the 1,400 km coastline it patrols has been under a maritime curfew since July 2014 — nearly 12 years of measures that affect B40 fishermen and women traders who depend on coastal economies. The government's exploration of mobile offshore platforms is pragmatic but also an acknowledgment that permanent infrastructure keeps failing to materialise in a region where 19.5% poverty dwarfs the national rate. Three successive administrations have maintained ESSCOM's temporary legal status. That is not one government's failure — it is a system that treats eastern Sabah's security needs as urgent during crises and optional between them. The people of eastern Sabah deserve both operational security and the institutional permanence that sustains it.
revisions: ADDED (Stage 4): Philippine military ops and COVID as contributing factors to zero kidnappings
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Cost overrun framed as '54% over original cost' using correct RM52.81M baseline
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Curfew since July 2014 (not 2013), framed as 'nearly 12 years'
ADDED (Stage 2/4): B40 fishermen and women traders impact of curfew
ADDED (Stage 4): Sabah poverty rate as structural context
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Three successive administrations named — systemic not partisan failure

sources: AG Report 2025 Series 3, MOHA official history, DOSM (2025), BERNAMA, PPDJS/ESSCOM (Apr 2025), CSIS AMTI (May 2025), FMT, The Star, The Edge, Borneo Post, Astro Awani, Utusan Malaysia, KKR Malaysia, LSE Southeast Asia Blog, Jesselton Times, SEARCCT

CORRECTED (Stage 3): ESSCOM established 1 April 2013 not 7 March
CORRECTED (Stage 3): FOB original cost RM52.81M not RM41M
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Semporna complex partial handover not completed
CORRECTED (Stage 3): US advisory — eastern Sabah islands remain Level 2
REMOVED (Stage 3): Unverified claim about permanent bill deferrals
ADDED (Stage 2): Gender dimension — women's livelihoods under curfew
ADDED (Stage 2): Balanced Sabah-KL framing with federal perspective
ADDED (Stage 2): Community agency for Sulu/Bajau/Filipino groups
ADDED (Stage 4): Philippine military + COVID as KFR contributing factors
ADDED (Stage 4): Sabah poverty rate 19.5% vs national 6.2%
ADDED (Stage 4): Balanced attribution of cost overruns (remote logistics)
ADDED (Stage 5): Cross-administration failure pattern (BN, PN, PH)
